title: Lung Cancer — NSCLC
oneLiner: Most common primary lung cancer (~85%), strongly linked to smoking, often advanced at diagnosis — 5-year survival ~15-20% overall but curative options exist for early stage.

sections.Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: **One-liner:** Most common primary lung cancer (~85%), strongly linked to smoking, often advanced at diagnosis — 5-year survival ~15-20% overall but curative options exist for early stage.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO)** — face/neck swelling, distended neck veins, stridor, headache, dilated chest wall veins. Key finding: **facial plethora and arm oedema that worsens when lying flat**. If suspected, start dexamethasone 8mg IV stat, call oncology/radiology for urgent CT venogram and stenting — do not wait for histology.
- **Massive haemoptysis** — >600mL blood in 24h or ongoing coughing of blood. Key finding: **frothy bright red blood with clots, inability to clear airway**. ABC: suction, lateral decubitus (bleeding side down), call anaesthetics/ICU; consider bronchial artery embolisation.
- **Malignant pericardial effusion / tamponade** — dyspnoea, hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus. Key finding: **tachycardia + low voltage QRS on ECG + enlarged cardiac silhouette on CXR** (water bottle heart). Urgent echo, call cardiology for drainage.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** persistent cough (>3 weeks), haemoptysis, unexplained dyspnoea, chest/shoulder pain, weight loss, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve), clubbing.
- **Key exam findings:** unilateral wheeze, reduced breath sounds, dullness to percussion (effusion/consolidation), supraclavicular lymph nodes, finger clubbing, Horner's syndrome (Pancoast tumour: ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis).
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes** (less common in NSCLC than SCLC): hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis), hypercalcaemia (squamous cell), SIADH, Cushing's (adenocarcinoma).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Community-acquired pneumonia** — fever, productive cough, consolidative signs; resolution with antibiotics on CXR (unlikely to be normal at 6-8 weeks).
- **Pulmonary tuberculosis** — night sweats, weight loss, apical infiltrates, positive IGRA/sputum AFB; consider if immunosuppressed or from endemic area.
- **Benign lung mass** (e.g., hamartoma, granuloma) — slow growth, often incidental, no nodal disease; CT features: popcorn calcification (hamartoma).
- **Pleural effusion (non-malignant)** — e.g., heart failure, TB, empyema; exudate/transudate criteria + cytology negative.
- **Lung metastasis from another primary** — known cancer history, multiple nodules, biopsy with IHC to confirm origin.
- **Splitter:** **Haemoptysis + CXR mass + clubbing** → NSCLC highly likely; absence of clubbing and presence of systemic fever favours infection.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** SpO2, ECG (check for arrhythmia, pericardial effusion), peak flow (if breathless), capillary blood gas if hypoxic.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (Ca²⁺ for hypercalcaemia), LDH, CRP, clotting screen, blood group and cross-match if haemoptysis. **Consider CT brain before PET-CT if any neurological symptoms.**
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Chest X-ray PA + lateral** (often first abnormality — mass, hilar enlargement, pleural effusion, collapse).
  - Second-line: **CT thorax with contrast** from lung apices to adrenal glands (assess tumour size, nodal stations, chest wall invasion, adrenal metastases).
  - **PET-CT** for staging if patient potentially fit for curative treatment (detects distant metastases).
  - **MRI brain** if neurological symptoms or headache (exclude brain metastases).
- **Special tests:** **Biopsy** — CT-guided (peripheral lesions), bronchoscopy with EBUS-TBNA (central/hilar/mediastinal nodes), endobronchial biopsy (endobronchial tumour). For pleural effusion: **ultrasound-guided pleural biopsy** + fluid cytology. **Histology** with molecular testing: EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, PD-L1 (determines targeted therapy and immunotherapy eligibility). **Sputum cytology** limited sensitivity.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **TNM staging (8th edition)** — imaging (CT/PET) ± mediastinal staging (EBUS-TBNA, mediastinoscopy). Key thresholds: N2 disease (ipsilateral mediastinal nodes) often precludes upfront surgery; M1 (distant metastasis) stage IV.
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy with molecular markers** — omission means missing potential targeted therapy (e.g., osimertinib if EGFR+) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if PD-L1 ≥50%).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If SVCO: **dexamethasone 8mg IV stat** (not needed if stent planned), call oncology/radiology for **urgent CT venogram + SVC stent**. Check hydration status.
  - If massive haemoptysis: **airway suction, IV access, cross-match 4 units, call ICU/anaesthetics**; position patient bleeding side down. Bronchial artery embolisation by interventional radiology.
  - If tamponade: **urgent echo, call cardiology for pericardiocentesis**.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Symptom management: **oxygen to SpO2 ≥94%**, analgesia (paracetamol ± codeine for cough); treat co-existing COPD/exacerbation with bronchodilators.
  - Perform CT staging, organise biopsy (bronchoscopy or CT-guided). Send samples for molecular testing (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, PD-L1). Discuss at **lung cancer MDT**.
  - If Stage I-IIIA and fit: referral to thoracic surgery (VATS lobectomy) or SABR (if poor lung function). If N2 disease: consider neoadjuvant chemotherapy (platinum doublet) or definitive chemoradiotherapy (for stage III).
  - If Stage IV: **first-line therapy depends on histology and molecular results** — if PD-L1 ≥50%: **pembrolizumab monotherapy**; if EGFR mutation: **osimertinib**; if ALK rearrangement: **alectinib**; otherwise platinum-based doublet chemotherapy ± pembrolizumab (if PD-L1 1-49%) or bevacizumab (non-squamous).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete treatment cycles (typically 4-6 cycles for chemotherapy; continuous for targeted/immunotherapy until progression). Restaging CT every 2-3 months.
  - Palliative radiotherapy for painful bone metastases, brain metastases (WBRT or SRS), or haemoptysis.
  - Best supportive care: community palliative team, pain/breathlessness management, steroids for brain oedema.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 5% dextrose or 0.9% normal saline at 1L/8h if nil by mouth for procedure; otherwise encourage oral intake.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **enoxaparin 40mg SC daily** (or 20mg if weight <50kg or CrCl <30) — cancer patients have high VTE risk.
  - **Diet:** Full diet unless NBM for biopsy/bronchoscopy.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly; if respiratory distress or haemoptysis, increase to hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (treatment protocols vary — [check local policy] for exact regimens):**
  - **Pembrolizumab** (if PD-L1 ≥50%): 200mg IV every 3 weeks (or 400mg every 6 weeks) until progression.
  - **Carboplatin + pemetrexed** (non-squamous): carboplatin AUC 5-6 IV day 1, pemetrexed 500mg/m² IV day 1 every 21 days, with folic acid + vitamin B12 premedication.
  - **Carboplatin + gemcitabine** (squamous): carboplatin AUC 5-6 IV day 1, gemcitabine 1250mg/m² IV days 1+8 every 21 days.
  - **Osimertinib** (EGFR mutation): 80mg PO once daily.
  - **Alectinib** (ALK rearrangement): 600mg PO twice daily.
- **Common alternatives:** cisplatin (requires pre-hydration, avoid if renal impairment); vinorelbine (oral or IV); docetaxel (second-line). Immunotherapy: nivolumab, atezolizumab.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Pembrolizumab**: avoid in active autoimmune disease, recent organ transplant, or interstitial lung disease.
  - **Osimertinib**: avoid strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin, phenytoin).
  - **Carboplatin**: dose adjust for CrCl <60 (use Calvert formula).
  - **Pemetrexed**: need folic acid 400-500mcg PO daily and vitamin B12 1000mcg IM every 9 weeks; reduce dose if CrCl <45 (contraindicated if CrCl <30).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: Calvert formula AUC×(CrCl+25) — check CrCl; avoid if CrCl <30.
  - Osimertinib: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal/hepatic; severe renal/hepatic — [check local policy].
  - Pembrolizumab: no dose adjustment in renal/hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Carboplatin — incorrect dose if CrCl overestimated or Calvert formula not used** (risk of fatal myelosuppression). Always double-check CrCl with IDMS-standardised creatinine and use Calvert formula (not BSA) for every cycle.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **SVCO** (new). **Massive haemoptysis**. **Post-obstructive pneumonia** (antibiotics). **Chemotherapy toxicities** (neutropenic sepsis [check FBC, rescuscitate], febrile neutropenia protocol; nausea/vomiting; nephrotoxicity with cisplatin; peripheral neuropathy with taxanes).
  - **Immunotherapy-related adverse events** (irAEs): pneumonitis (cough, dyspnoea, new infiltrates — hold immunotherapy, start prednisolone 1-2mg/kg), colitis (diarrhoea), hepatitis, skin rash, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis). Check thyroid function every cycle.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Malignant pleural effusion** (recurrent, requires pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter). **Superior sulcus syndrome** (Pancoast) — persistent arm pain, Horner's, brachial plexopathy. **Radiotherapy fibrosis** (dyspnoea, cough). **Brain metastases** (headache, focal neurology). **Cachexia** (nutritional support, megestrol acetate if appropriate).
sections.Prognosis:
- Stage IA (T1N0) : 5-year survival ~75-80% after surgery.
- Stage IIIA (N2) : 5-year survival ~15-25% after chemoradiotherapy.
- Stage IV (any metastasis) : median survival ~12-18 months with modern therapy (longer with targeted/immunotherapy). Poor performance status (ECOG ≥3) → median <3 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check performance status (ECOG 0-2) before any treatment decision** — poor PS often dictates best supportive care only; good PS can tolerate aggressive therapy.
- **Always obtain tissue for molecular testing** in NSCLC — even if biopsy is small, ask pathology to save a core for next-gen sequencing. Missing EGFR/ALK could deny the patient months of oral targeted therapy.
- **If patient has new persistent cough >3 weeks and is a smoker >45 years, order CXR even if normal exam** — GPs should do this (NICE NG12).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't rush to treat with chemotherapy before MDT discussion and molecular results** — targeted therapy may be much better tolerated and more effective.
- **Avoid using oral steroids without first checking for TB or fungal infection** (especially if from endemic area) — can reactivate latent infection.
- **Never give growth factors (G-CSF) concurrently with chemotherapy** — increases thrombocytopenia risk. Give 24h after chemo end.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New onset of haemoptysis (any amount), worsening shortness of breath, facial/arm swelling (SVCO), new neurological symptoms (headache, seizure, weakness), fever >38°C with chest pain (post-obstructive pneumonia).
- **Follow-up:** Lung cancer clinical nurse specialist (CNS) within 1 week of diagnosis; MDT review of imaging/biopsy results within 14 days. If on immunotherapy, urine dipstick and TFTs each cycle.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU:** if oxygen requirement >4L/min to maintain SpO2 ≥92%, or rising NEWS2 ≥5 despite initial treatment. For massive haemoptysis, call anaesthetics and interventional radiology immediately. For suspected airway obstruction (stridor), call anaesthetics for airway assessment.
- **Specific thresholds:** **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis/severe illness). **GCS ≤14** or new neurological deficit (urgent CT brain and neurosurgery/oncology reg). **SBP <90 mmHg** or **HR >130** (sepsis, tamponade, PE).

rawMarkdown:
## Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)
**One-liner:** Most common primary lung cancer (~85%), strongly linked to smoking, often advanced at diagnosis — 5-year survival ~15-20% overall but curative options exist for early stage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO)** — face/neck swelling, distended neck veins, stridor, headache, dilated chest wall veins. Key finding: **facial plethora and arm oedema that worsens when lying flat**. If suspected, start dexamethasone 8mg IV stat, call oncology/radiology for urgent CT venogram and stenting — do not wait for histology.
- **Massive haemoptysis** — >600mL blood in 24h or ongoing coughing of blood. Key finding: **frothy bright red blood with clots, inability to clear airway**. ABC: suction, lateral decubitus (bleeding side down), call anaesthetics/ICU; consider bronchial artery embolisation.
- **Malignant pericardial effusion / tamponade** — dyspnoea, hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus. Key finding: **tachycardia + low voltage QRS on ECG + enlarged cardiac silhouette on CXR** (water bottle heart). Urgent echo, call cardiology for drainage.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** persistent cough (>3 weeks), haemoptysis, unexplained dyspnoea, chest/shoulder pain, weight loss, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve), clubbing.
- **Key exam findings:** unilateral wheeze, reduced breath sounds, dullness to percussion (effusion/consolidation), supraclavicular lymph nodes, finger clubbing, Horner's syndrome (Pancoast tumour: ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis).
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes** (less common in NSCLC than SCLC): hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis), hypercalcaemia (squamous cell), SIADH, Cushing's (adenocarcinoma).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Community-acquired pneumonia** — fever, productive cough, consolidative signs; resolution with antibiotics on CXR (unlikely to be normal at 6-8 weeks).
- **Pulmonary tuberculosis** — night sweats, weight loss, apical infiltrates, positive IGRA/sputum AFB; consider if immunosuppressed or from endemic area.
- **Benign lung mass** (e.g., hamartoma, granuloma) — slow growth, often incidental, no nodal disease; CT features: popcorn calcification (hamartoma).
- **Pleural effusion (non-malignant)** — e.g., heart failure, TB, empyema; exudate/transudate criteria + cytology negative.
- **Lung metastasis from another primary** — known cancer history, multiple nodules, biopsy with IHC to confirm origin.
- **Splitter:** **Haemoptysis + CXR mass + clubbing** → NSCLC highly likely; absence of clubbing and presence of systemic fever favours infection.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** SpO2, ECG (check for arrhythmia, pericardial effusion), peak flow (if breathless), capillary blood gas if hypoxic.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (Ca²⁺ for hypercalcaemia), LDH, CRP, clotting screen, blood group and cross-match if haemoptysis. **Consider CT brain before PET-CT if any neurological symptoms.**
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Chest X-ray PA + lateral** (often first abnormality — mass, hilar enlargement, pleural effusion, collapse).
  - Second-line: **CT thorax with contrast** from lung apices to adrenal glands (assess tumour size, nodal stations, chest wall invasion, adrenal metastases).
  - **PET-CT** for staging if patient potentially fit for curative treatment (detects distant metastases).
  - **MRI brain** if neurological symptoms or headache (exclude brain metastases).
- **Special tests:** **Biopsy** — CT-guided (peripheral lesions), bronchoscopy with EBUS-TBNA (central/hilar/mediastinal nodes), endobronchial biopsy (endobronchial tumour). For pleural effusion: **ultrasound-guided pleural biopsy** + fluid cytology. **Histology** with molecular testing: EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, PD-L1 (determines targeted therapy and immunotherapy eligibility). **Sputum cytology** limited sensitivity.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **TNM staging (8th edition)** — imaging (CT/PET) ± mediastinal staging (EBUS-TBNA, mediastinoscopy). Key thresholds: N2 disease (ipsilateral mediastinal nodes) often precludes upfront surgery; M1 (distant metastasis) stage IV.
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy with molecular markers** — omission means missing potential targeted therapy (e.g., osimertinib if EGFR+) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if PD-L1 ≥50%).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If SVCO: **dexamethasone 8mg IV stat** (not needed if stent planned), call oncology/radiology for **urgent CT venogram + SVC stent**. Check hydration status.
  - If massive haemoptysis: **airway suction, IV access, cross-match 4 units, call ICU/anaesthetics**; position patient bleeding side down. Bronchial artery embolisation by interventional radiology.
  - If tamponade: **urgent echo, call cardiology for pericardiocentesis**.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Symptom management: **oxygen to SpO2 ≥94%**, analgesia (paracetamol ± codeine for cough); treat co-existing COPD/exacerbation with bronchodilators.
  - Perform CT staging, organise biopsy (bronchoscopy or CT-guided). Send samples for molecular testing (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, PD-L1). Discuss at **lung cancer MDT**.
  - If Stage I-IIIA and fit: referral to thoracic surgery (VATS lobectomy) or SABR (if poor lung function). If N2 disease: consider neoadjuvant chemotherapy (platinum doublet) or definitive chemoradiotherapy (for stage III).
  - If Stage IV: **first-line therapy depends on histology and molecular results** — if PD-L1 ≥50%: **pembrolizumab monotherapy**; if EGFR mutation: **osimertinib**; if ALK rearrangement: **alectinib**; otherwise platinum-based doublet chemotherapy ± pembrolizumab (if PD-L1 1-49%) or bevacizumab (non-squamous).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete treatment cycles (typically 4-6 cycles for chemotherapy; continuous for targeted/immunotherapy until progression). Restaging CT every 2-3 months.
  - Palliative radiotherapy for painful bone metastases, brain metastases (WBRT or SRS), or haemoptysis.
  - Best supportive care: community palliative team, pain/breathlessness management, steroids for brain oedema.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 5% dextrose or 0.9% normal saline at 1L/8h if nil by mouth for procedure; otherwise encourage oral intake.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **enoxaparin 40mg SC daily** (or 20mg if weight <50kg or CrCl <30) — cancer patients have high VTE risk.
  - **Diet:** Full diet unless NBM for biopsy/bronchoscopy.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly; if respiratory distress or haemoptysis, increase to hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (treatment protocols vary — [check local policy] for exact regimens):**
  - **Pembrolizumab** (if PD-L1 ≥50%): 200mg IV every 3 weeks (or 400mg every 6 weeks) until progression.
  - **Carboplatin + pemetrexed** (non-squamous): carboplatin AUC 5-6 IV day 1, pemetrexed 500mg/m² IV day 1 every 21 days, with folic acid + vitamin B12 premedication.
  - **Carboplatin + gemcitabine** (squamous): carboplatin AUC 5-6 IV day 1, gemcitabine 1250mg/m² IV days 1+8 every 21 days.
  - **Osimertinib** (EGFR mutation): 80mg PO once daily.
  - **Alectinib** (ALK rearrangement): 600mg PO twice daily.
- **Common alternatives:** cisplatin (requires pre-hydration, avoid if renal impairment); vinorelbine (oral or IV); docetaxel (second-line). Immunotherapy: nivolumab, atezolizumab.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Pembrolizumab**: avoid in active autoimmune disease, recent organ transplant, or interstitial lung disease.
  - **Osimertinib**: avoid strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin, phenytoin).
  - **Carboplatin**: dose adjust for CrCl <60 (use Calvert formula).
  - **Pemetrexed**: need folic acid 400-500mcg PO daily and vitamin B12 1000mcg IM every 9 weeks; reduce dose if CrCl <45 (contraindicated if CrCl <30).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: Calvert formula AUC×(CrCl+25) — check CrCl; avoid if CrCl <30.
  - Osimertinib: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal/hepatic; severe renal/hepatic — [check local policy].
  - Pembrolizumab: no dose adjustment in renal/hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Carboplatin — incorrect dose if CrCl overestimated or Calvert formula not used** (risk of fatal myelosuppression). Always double-check CrCl with IDMS-standardised creatinine and use Calvert formula (not BSA) for every cycle.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **SVCO** (new). **Massive haemoptysis**. **Post-obstructive pneumonia** (antibiotics). **Chemotherapy toxicities** (neutropenic sepsis [check FBC, rescuscitate], febrile neutropenia protocol; nausea/vomiting; nephrotoxicity with cisplatin; peripheral neuropathy with taxanes).
  - **Immunotherapy-related adverse events** (irAEs): pneumonitis (cough, dyspnoea, new infiltrates — hold immunotherapy, start prednisolone 1-2mg/kg), colitis (diarrhoea), hepatitis, skin rash, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis). Check thyroid function every cycle.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Malignant pleural effusion** (recurrent, requires pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter). **Superior sulcus syndrome** (Pancoast) — persistent arm pain, Horner's, brachial plexopathy. **Radiotherapy fibrosis** (dyspnoea, cough). **Brain metastases** (headache, focal neurology). **Cachexia** (nutritional support, megestrol acetate if appropriate).

### Prognosis
- Stage IA (T1N0) : 5-year survival ~75-80% after surgery.
- Stage IIIA (N2) : 5-year survival ~15-25% after chemoradiotherapy.
- Stage IV (any metastasis) : median survival ~12-18 months with modern therapy (longer with targeted/immunotherapy). Poor performance status (ECOG ≥3) → median <3 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check performance status (ECOG 0-2) before any treatment decision** — poor PS often dictates best supportive care only; good PS can tolerate aggressive therapy.
- **Always obtain tissue for molecular testing** in NSCLC — even if biopsy is small, ask pathology to save a core for next-gen sequencing. Missing EGFR/ALK could deny the patient months of oral targeted therapy.
- **If patient has new persistent cough >3 weeks and is a smoker >45 years, order CXR even if normal exam** — GPs should do this (NICE NG12).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't rush to treat with chemotherapy before MDT discussion and molecular results** — targeted therapy may be much better tolerated and more effective.
- **Avoid using oral steroids without first checking for TB or fungal infection** (especially if from endemic area) — can reactivate latent infection.
- **Never give growth factors (G-CSF) concurrently with chemotherapy** — increases thrombocytopenia risk. Give 24h after chemo end.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New onset of haemoptysis (any amount), worsening shortness of breath, facial/arm swelling (SVCO), new neurological symptoms (headache, seizure, weakness), fever >38°C with chest pain (post-obstructive pneumonia).
- **Follow-up:** Lung cancer clinical nurse specialist (CNS) within 1 week of diagnosis; MDT review of imaging/biopsy results within 14 days. If on immunotherapy, urine dipstick and TFTs each cycle.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU:** if oxygen requirement >4L/min to maintain SpO2 ≥92%, or rising NEWS2 ≥5 despite initial treatment. For massive haemoptysis, call anaesthetics and interventional radiology immediately. For suspected airway obstruction (stridor), call anaesthetics for airway assessment.
- **Specific thresholds:** **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** (sepsis/severe illness). **GCS ≤14** or new neurological deficit (urgent CT brain and neurosurgery/oncology reg). **SBP <90 mmHg** or **HR >130** (sepsis, tamponade, PE).
